data.title: Friday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment

The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.

The preliminary reading was shockingly low at 47.6 for April, so forecasters see a little recovery to 48.0 in the final April report, still remarkably weak versus 53.3 in March and down about 9 percent from a year ago. Consumers remain upset about rising prices for gas and other essentials and more fearful about jobs with the energy shock from the Iran war. 

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

data.title: Knowles forecasts Q2 2026 EPS of $0.28 to $0.32 as Precision Devices ramps energy order by end of Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Knowles Corporation (KN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We started 2026 with solid financial results in Q1 and great momentum entering the rest of the year." (President, CEO & Director Jeffrey Niew)

  • "We had strong organic growth in the first quarter as we delivered revenue of $153 million, up 16% year-over-year and at the high end of our guided range." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "EPS of $0.27, up 50% year-over-year, exceeded the high end of our guided range and cash utilized in operations of $1 million was within our guided range." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "In Q1, Medtech & Specialty Audio revenue was $68 million, up 14% year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "In the Precision Devices segment, Q1 revenues was $85 million, up 17% year-over-year with all our end markets we serve, Medtech, Defense, Industrial and Electrification growing on a year-over-year basis." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "In addition to the strong shipments we saw in the first quarter, our book-to-bill in Precision Devices was very strong at 1.19." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "With a very healthy backlog of existing orders, we now expect our revenue growth in 2026 to be above the high end of our target organic revenue target of 4% to 6% that we provided at our Investor Day in May of last year." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • "We reported first quarter revenues of $153 million, up 16% from the year ago period and at the high end of our guidance range." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer John Anderson)

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, revenues are expected to be between $152 million and $162 million." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We're projecting EPS to be within a range of $0.28 to $0.32 per share." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We're projecting adjusted EBIT margin for the quarter to be within the range of 20% to 22%." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We're projecting cash from operating activities to be within the range of $20 million to $30 million." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We expect full year capital spending to be approximately 4% to 5% of revenues as we continue to make investments in the first half of this year associated with capacity expansion related to the large energy order we received in 2025." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "Therefore, we are projecting Medtech & Specialty Audio will grow within the 2% to 4% range for the full year 2026." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

Financial Results

  • "In the Medtech & Specialty Audio segment, Q1 revenue was $68 million, up 14% compared with the year ago period" and "Q1 margin -- gross margins were 53.5%, up 480 basis points from the year ago period." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "For full year 2026, we expect MSA gross margins to be in line with 2025 margins of 51%." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "The Precision Devices segment delivered first quarter revenues of $85 million" and "Segment gross margins were 39.2%, up 350 basis points from the first quarter of 2025." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "These improvements were partially offset by higher factory costs in our specialty film product line as we ramp up production capacity to support our $75 million-plus energy order." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "During the first quarter, we repurchased 276,000 shares at a total cost of $7.5 million." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • "We exited the quarter with cash of $41 million and $131 million of borrowings under our revolving credit facility." (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

Q&A

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: asked for highlights on "numerous design wins" and lifetime value; President, CEO & Director Niew responded, "the energy order... will be fully ramped up at the end of Q2" and added, "orders are up" in defense, with "strength in '27... shaping up to be even better for defense."

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: asked about pricing power and gross-margin drivers; President, CEO & Director Niew said in Precision Devices, "we should lead us in that 2% to 4% in the PD business on price a year," while Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson said PD margin expansion is expected as "we see increasing capacity utilization... in the back half of '26."

  • Will Gildea, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked on specialty film pilots timing and new pilots; President, CEO & Director Niew said, "we're due to deliver pilots on 20 different customers over the next, say, quarter" and added on the energy order, "we are on track from a ramp standpoint" and "on track from a yield standpoint."

  • Will Gildea, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked about war-related headwinds; Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson said, "Just a little bit on input costs" including "some resin-based products, some modest increases, but not significant," while President, CEO & Director Niew added, "a lot of our customers take possession at our dock, and we're not paying for the shipping anyway."

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked if 2027 pricing/gross margins could be higher; President, CEO & Director Niew said, "No," adding PD pricing follows "our kind of... playbook," while Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson said, "there is some opportunity in '27 to be above '26" because "'27 margins should be similar to what the margins we exit '26 at."

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked on remaining energy ramp hurdles; President, CEO & Director Niew said, "it's a lot of blocking and tackling" and "All the equipment is on site."

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked about satellite exposure in RF; President, CEO & Director Niew said, "we do have some satellite business" but emphasized focus on "super, super high-performance RF filters" and added, "we don't really want to be in the commoditized portions of the market."

  • Tristan Gerra, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: asked about utilization and energy-ramp margin impact; President, CEO & Director Niew said PD is "probably running on average in the 80% range," while Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson added, "think of maybe 200 to 250 basis points better than we're doing today" for PD margin trajectory tied "heavily by this energy order."

  • Tristan Gerra, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated, Research Division: asked about customers securing 2027 capacity; President, CEO & Director Niew said in defense there are discussions to place "a 3- or a 5-year order" and added "97% of that book-to-bill will be shipped within 12 months."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, with repeated probing on durability of demand and margins, including questions on "pricing power," energy-ramp risks, and whether customers are trying to "secure capacity into '27." (Christopher Rolland, Anthony Stoss, Tristan Gerra)

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more detailed in Q&A, including "great momentum" and "confidence" language; when pressed on 2027 pricing, President, CEO & Director Niew was direct: "No." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • Compared with the prior quarter, both calls emphasized backlog and the specialty film/energy ramp; Q1 introduced a more explicit acceleration message: "we now expect our revenue growth in 2026 to be above the high end" of the 4% to 6% target. (President, CEO & Director Niew)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 reiterated and tightened focus on the energy order timeline, saying it "will be fully ramped up at the end of Q2" and that it was "not a significant contributor in Q1," aligning with Q4’s view that the program would be "fully ramped in Q2" and weighted to the back half. (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • Q1 emphasized defense demand visibility into 2027 more explicitly, stating "we do expect increasing demand in 2027 and beyond" and discussing potential "3- or a 5-year order" conversations; Q4 referenced defense strength but focused more on broad end-market strength and normalized channel inventory. (President, CEO & Director Niew)

  • Q1 highlighted a stronger PD ordering signal with "book-to-bill... 1.19" versus Q4’s "book-to-bill was 1.06" and added, "This marked the sixth consecutive quarter where the book-to-bill was greater than 1." (President, CEO & Director Niew)

Risks and Concerns

  • "While we delivered significant year-over-year margin improvement in the first quarter" PD results were "partially offset by higher factory costs in our specialty film product line" during the ramp, with margin recovery tied to "the second half of 2026" as volume increases. (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson)

  • On geopolitical and supply impacts, management cited "some resin-based products, some modest increases, but not significant" and pointed to limited transportation exposure given customer shipping terms. (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer Anderson; President, CEO & Director Niew)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a high-end quarter on revenue with stronger-than-guided EPS and pointed to sustained demand signals in Precision Devices, including a 1.19 book-to-bill and rising defense activity. For Q2, management guided revenue of $152 million to $162 million and EPS of $0.28 to $0.32, while repeatedly tying second-half margin improvement to the specialty film energy-order ramp reaching full ramp by the end of Q2 and higher capacity utilization thereafter.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Microsoft offers buyouts to 7% of U.S. workforce amid AI shift: report
data.description:
• Microsoft (MSFT) is offering voluntary buyouts to a portion of its U.S. workforce - marking a first for the 51-year-old software giant - as the tech industry navigates significant shifts driven by the rise of artificial intelligence. According to an internal memo obtained by CNBC and Bloomberg, the company extended buyout offers to roughly 7% of its U.S. employees on Thursday.
  • Microsoft employed roughly 228,000 people as of June 2025, and of those, 125,000 were in the U.S., according to a Securities and Exchange Commission filing.
  • Those eligible for the buyouts include workers at the senior director level and below and employees whose years of employment at Microsoft and age add up to 70 or more.
  • Details for the buyouts will be disclosed to eligible employees and their managers on May 7, CNBC reported. 
  • The development comes as Meta Platforms (META) has confirmed to its employees that it will conduct job cuts next month and will close 6,000 open roles in an effort to increase efficiencies and offset heavy spending on its artificial intelligence ambitions.

data.title: Digital Realty signals 2026 core FFO of $8.00-$8.10 while scaling 1.2 GW under construction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Digital Realty Trust (DLR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Digital Realty got off to a record start in the first quarter of 2026" and "we continue to gain market share in our 0-1 plus interconnection product category" while also "providing needed hyperscale capacity in our greater than a megawatt category," according to "President, CEO & Director Andrew Power".
  • Power highlighted new leasing scale and mix: "We signed over $700 million of new leases in the quarter" and "a record 21% of 0-1 megawatt bookings were AI-oriented requirements," plus "116 new logos added in the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Power).
  • On hyperscale, Power said, "we signed the largest single lease in Digital Realty history this quarter, a 200-megawatt AI inference-oriented lease with a AA-rated hyperscaler in Charlotte," and added, "we launched construction on another 200-megawatt development site in Atlanta" (President, CEO & Director Power).
  • Mercier tied results to demand and balance sheet positioning: "we are raising our 2026 core FFO per share guidance range by $0.10 to $8 to $8.10 per share" and "debt to adjusted EBITDA dipped to 4.7x at quarter end" (Chief Financial Officer Matt Mercier).

Outlook

  • "We are raising our 2026 core FFO per share guidance range by $0.10 to $8 to $8.10 per share" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • "We also expect cash renewal spreads of 6.5% to 8.5%" and "power-based occupancy is still expected to improve by 50 to 100 basis points from year-end 2025" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • "Same capital cash NOI growth of 4% to 5% on a constant currency basis" and "CapEx net of partner contributions are poised to increase ... to a range of $3.5 billion to $4 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the company moved from "a core FFO guidance range for the full year 2026 of $7.90 to $8 per share" to $8.00-$8.10, and from "cash renewal spreads of between 6% to 8%" to 6.5%-8.5% (Chief Financial Officer Mercier, prior-quarter transcript vs. current).

Financial Results

  • "Core FFO of $2.04 per share for the first quarter" and "same capital cash NOI growth ... increasing by 7.9% year-over-year" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • "Total backlog ... reached a new record of $1.8 billion" and "at Digital Realty's share, the backlog reached a new record of $1 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • Development and returns: "we spent $910 million on development CapEx in the quarter" and "delivered 63 megawatts of new capacity, 84% of which was pre-leased" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • Pipeline expansion: "started about 464 megawatts of new data center capacity" and "increasing our total development to 1.2 gigawatts under construction"; "our gross data center pipeline under construction stood at approximately $16.5 billion" and was "nearly 80% ... situated in the Americas region" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).

Q&A

  • Erik Rasmussen, Stifel: Asked about AI deal economics, escalators, and training vs. inference inflection; President, CEO & Director Power replied, "our hyperscale contracts are, call it, 15 years and escalators are certainly 3% or maybe even higher," and Chief Technology Officer Chris Sharp added, "demand has definitely converted from pilot to production" and "as agents come to market, it's a demand multiplier. And so that represents to us a 5x to 30x more tokens per task".
  • Frank Louthan, Raymond James: Asked about Atlanta land bank and power timing; Chief Investment Officer Gregory Wright said, "it's, call it, north of 870 acres" and "we're still working through things with the power company".
  • Matthew Niknam, Truist: Asked about longer commencement lag; Chief Financial Officer Mercier said it was "driven by what was our largest lease this quarter" and that the 200-megawatt project "will be delivering over a phased period starting next year into '28".
  • Jonathan Petersen, Jefferies: Asked what drove OpEx pressure; President, CEO & Director Power said, "it was largely a result of a low operating expense comp in the prior year same quarter" and "driven by R&M and labor largely".
  • Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo: Asked about delays/constraints and rent vs. cost inflation; President, CEO & Director Power said, "we're still seeing market rent growth outpacing inflationary pressure in build costs" and cited "competition over supply chain, labor" and "shortages of skilled labor electricians".
  • Michael Rollins, Citi: Asked why growth slows from Q1 pace into rest of year; Chief Financial Officer Mercier cited "OpEx" ramp, "increase in our development spend," potential land, and "capital recycling".
  • Timothy Horan, Oppenheimer: Asked for inventory/runway; President, CEO & Director Power said, "just shy roughly 3 gigawatts is operating today" and "we have another 6 gigawatts that we own today" with "1.2 gigawatts" under construction.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, repeatedly opening with congratulations while probing constraints and cadence, including "data center delays" and a question on why "Core FFO per share need to slow" (Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo; Michael Rollins, Citi).
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and confident in delivery, including "record start" and "raising our 2026 core FFO"; responses in Q&A stayed explanatory on cost and schedule pressures, including "competition over supply chain, labor" (President, CEO & Director Power; Chief Financial Officer Mercier).
  • Versus Q4 2025, the current call contained more specific scale disclosures around AI and hyperscale (for example, "200-megawatt" Charlotte) and a firmer near-term guidance action ("raising" the range), while Q4 framed 2026 as "establishing" the initial range (President, CEO & Director Power; Chief Financial Officer Mercier).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from "$7.90 to $8 per share" to "$8 to $8.10 per share" for 2026 core FFO, and renewal spreads from "6% to 8%" to "6.5% to 8.5%" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).
  • Backlog increased from "nearly $1.4 billion" at year-end to "$1.8 billion" in Q1, while development under construction rose from "769 megawatts" to "1.2 gigawatts" and pipeline value from "just over $10 billion" to "approximately $16.5 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).
  • Balance sheet language stayed focused on leverage discipline, moving from "Leverage remained at 4.9x" to "4.7x" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).

Risks and Concerns

  • Power identified industry constraints: "power availability, labor and supply chain risks and community concerns" (President, CEO & Director Power).
  • On delivery and costs, management referenced "utility partners that may have delays" and "tightness on supply chains" (President, CEO & Director Power).
  • Mercier flagged macro inputs: "Given the conflict in the Middle East, energy costs and supply chain risks are once again in the spotlight," while adding, "approximately 90% of our utility expense is reimbursed by customers" and "a large majority of our electricity is hedged forward through 2026 and beyond" (Chief Financial Officer Mercier).

Final Takeaway

Digital Realty’s management described Q1 2026 as a record start driven by both enterprise and hyperscale demand, including a 200-megawatt AI inference lease in Charlotte, record 0-1 megawatt plus interconnection bookings, and a backlog that reached $1.8 billion. With leverage at 4.7x and an expanding development program, the company raised 2026 core FFO guidance to $8.00-$8.10, while acknowledging ongoing constraints in power, labor, supply chain, and local community pushback.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Newmont expects Cadia to return to 80% capacity in 5 weeks and maintains 2026 guidance with new $6B buyback authorization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Newmont (NEM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Newmont's focus on operational excellence continues to deliver consistent and predictable performance with our first quarter results demonstrating that we are on track to achieve our 2026 guidance." (CEO, President & Director Natascha Viljoen)
  • "The benefits of record free cash flow generation are flowing through our enhanced capital allocation framework, resulting in continuous reinvestment in our business, a predictable quarterly dividend and ongoing share repurchases, supplemented by new $6 billion dollar share repurchase authorization." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • "We are currently processing surface stockpiles and expect underground rehabilitation to be completed in the next 5 weeks, enabling return to 80% operating capacity with full recovery expected by the end of the second quarter." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • "In the first quarter, we produced 1.3 million ounces of gold, 30,000 tonnes of copper and 9 million ounces of silver" and "$3.8 billion in cash flow from operations after working capital and $3.1 billion in free cash flow, marking another all-time quarterly record." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • "Newmont delivered outstanding financial results in the first quarter" and "adjusted EBITDA of $5.2 billion and adjusted net income of $2.90 per diluted share" with "Gold all-in sustaining costs... at $1,029 per ounce for the first quarter on a byproduct basis." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Peter Wexler)

Outlook

  • "We continue to monitor the geopolitical environment and its potential impact on costs closely" and "are, therefore, maintaining our full year cost guidance at this time." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • "Looking ahead to the second quarter, we expect production to be slightly below the first quarter, keeping us on track to deliver our full year production guidance of 5.3 million ounces." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • "All-in sustaining costs are expected to be notably higher in the second quarter and more in line with the guidance we provided in February." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • "We believe it is appropriate to maintain our existing production weighting" and "Our first quarter outperformance provides prudent flexibility to absorb any impact from temporary interruptions to mill feed at Cadia in the second quarter." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted EBITDA of $5.2 billion" and "adjusted net income of $2.90 per diluted share" were supported by "$3.8 billion in cash flow from operations after working capital" and "a record $3.1 billion of free cash flow even after making approximately $1.3 billion in cash tax payments during the quarter." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • "We also received approximately $321 million in after-tax proceeds from the sale of equity investments in SolGold and Greatland resources" and "bringing total after-tax proceeds received from our noncore divestiture program to over $4.6 billion." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • "Since our last earnings call, we have repurchased $2.4 billion in shares" and "bringing the total repurchases to $6 billion since we began repurchasing shares over 24 months ago." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • "In the first quarter, we declared a dividend of $0.26 per share" and "we continue to invest in sustaining capital... with $381 million spent in the first quarter" plus "$239 million deployed" in development capital. (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)

Q&A

  • Tanya Jakusconek, Scotiabank Global Banking and Markets, Research Division: "can you comment on... the default that was issued on February... with respect to Nevada Gold Mines?" Peter Wexler: "The period of the notice of default is open ended" and "there's no set time line for bringing it to resolution but we hope to do so in the near term." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • Matthew Murphy, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: "where operations were beating your expectations" and whether Q2 is the low point; Natascha Viljoen: "a slightly lower quarter as expected" and "we'll see the third quarter coming back stronger." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • Matthew Murphy, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: "any update" on the CFO search; Natascha Viljoen: "That recruitment process is going well... and we trust that we'll be able to share more information soon." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • Lawson Winder, BofA Securities, Research Division: levers to manage inflation; Natascha Viljoen: "The levers sits in 2 areas" including "productivity" and "cost discipline" and "we have a really strong supply chain team." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • Lawson Winder, BofA Securities, Research Division: "What's Newmont's appetite for acquisitions"; Natascha Viljoen: "Our focus at this stage remains firmly internally on our own operations." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • Fahad Tariq, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: Ghana contractor mining headline; Natascha Viljoen: "We are in active engagement" and "we will -- we are following a process that is commercially and technically disciplined." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • Daniel Major, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: medium-term guidance and 2027 direction; Natascha Viljoen: "2026, we run a trough" and "as we work through this year, we will... consider that for 2027 guidance." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • Bob Brackett, Bernstein Institutional Services LLC, Research Division: remedies under NGM default; Peter Wexler: "there are a range of possibilities" and "we are working through the structured process in the JV agreement." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)

Sentiment Analysis

  • "I'm just trying to understand how long this process is going to take" and "what are your rights under the JV" reflected a slightly negative, pressing analyst tone around NGM governance and timing. (Tanya Jakusconek, Scotiabank Global Banking and Markets, Research Division; Bob Brackett, Bernstein Institutional Services LLC, Research Division)
  • "We hope to do so in the near term" and "we hope it's the former" reflected a neutral-to-slightly positive management tone, emphasizing process and resolution without committing to a timeline. (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • "At this time, the only information available to us is what has been publicly disclosed" in Q4 shifted in Q1 to "exercising our audit rights" and "reviewing those findings," indicating a more engaged posture in the current quarter discussion. (CEO, President & Director Viljoen; EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "We have issued a notice of default... [and] confidentiality provisions... prevent further comment" shifted to "exercising our audit rights" and "there's no set time line... but we hope to do so in the near term." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen; EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • Q4 introduced the shareholder-return structure: "today, we are introducing an enhanced capital allocation framework"; Q1 emphasized execution and expansion of repurchases: "our Board has doubled the size of our share repurchase program with an additional $6 billion authorization." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen; EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • Q4 expected "first quarter free cash flow to be lower than the fourth quarter of 2025"; Q1 reported "another all-time quarterly record" free cash flow. (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Second quarter production is expected to be lower" following "the magnitude 4.5 earthquake" at Cadia, with mitigation via "processing surface stockpiles" and a timeline where "full recovery [is] expected by the end of the second quarter." (CEO, President & Director Viljoen)
  • "Notable increase in energy prices" and "impacts to global supply chain dynamics" were flagged, with mitigation including "actively working to mitigate the impact" and leveraging "our scale and strong supply chain team." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)
  • "The newly introduced Ghana sliding scale royalty... incremental cost headwind of approximately $25 per ounce in 2026" with a stated mitigation goal: "mitigate the impact through disciplined cost management and productivity initiatives." (EVP, Chief Legal Officer & Interim CFO Wexler)

Final Takeaway

Newmont framed Q1 around record free cash flow and capital returns, including a new $6 billion repurchase authorization, while maintaining 2026 guidance despite near-term operational disruption at Cadia and rising energy-price volatility. Management positioned Q2 as temporarily lower—driven by Cadia rehabilitation and planned sequencing—while emphasizing a return toward normal operations by end of Q2 and stronger production into Q3, alongside continued focus on cost discipline, productivity, and an “open ended” but active process around the Nevada Gold Mines notice of default.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Five Point reaffirms ~$100M 2026 net income outlook while authorizing $40M share repurchase
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Five Point Holdings (FPH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “As expected, we began 2026 with a relatively quiet quarter from a land sales perspective and reported a consolidated net loss of $5 million.” (President & CEO Daniel Hedigan)

  • “From a revenue standpoint, we generated $13.6 million during the quarter, primarily from management services associated with our Great Park and Hearthstone segments.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • “We ended the quarter with total liquidity of $550.1 million, including $332 million of cash and cash equivalents.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • “The repurchase has been structured to preserve financial flexibility.” (President & CEO Hedigan) Hedigan said the board approved “the $40 million share repurchase” and tied it to valuation, saying shares were “trading at a significant discount to book value.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • Community operating update centered on buyer activity and land-sale timing: “the Great Park builder sold 82 homes during the quarter, while Valencia saw 90 home sales,” alongside plans for additional openings and land sales. (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • Growth platform update emphasized Hearthstone scale and new capital: “we successfully closed 2 new funds for a total of $600 million in new equity commitments,” and “This will provide the platform with the ability to deploy approximately $1 billion in capital with leverage.” (COO, Chief Legal Officer & VP Michael Alvarado)

  • Hearthstone positioning and footprint were reiterated: “The Hearthstone platform currently has approximately $3.4 billion in assets under management and has over 30,000 homesites under control with 13 homebuilders in 16 states.” (COO Alvarado)

  • CFO walked through quarter components: “We had $13 million of management services revenue,” including “$6.9 million associated with our management of the Great Park Venture” and “$6.1 million associated with Hearthstone.” (CFO, VP & Treasurer Kim Tobler)

Outlook

  • “We are reaffirming our guidance and continue to expect consolidated net income in 2026 to be approximately $100 million with our earnings weighted more heavily towards the second half of the year.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • Management linked the back-half weighting to transaction timing and fees: “we continue to expect our earnings in 2026 to be weighted towards the third and fourth quarters as land sales close and fee-based income from Hearthstone grows.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • Capital return and liquidity expectations were paired: “we anticipate that we will finish the year with more than $300 million in cash and total liquidity of over $500 million even after taking share repurchases into account.” (CFO Tobler)

  • Relative to the prior quarter’s call, the headline 2026 net income target stayed the same, but the current call added a specific capital action, with management announcing “the $40 million share repurchase.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

Financial results

  • “As expected, this was a quiet quarter without land sales, and we recognized a small loss of $5 million.” (CFO Tobler)

  • Expense and profitability bridge items included: “Our first quarter SG&A was $14.7 million,” “We recognized a small loss from our unconsolidated entities of $145,000,” and “Finally, we recognized $900,000 of tax benefit.” (CFO Tobler)

  • Liquidity and leverage metrics were reiterated with specific figures: “we ended the quarter with $332.6 million of cash as well as $217.5 million of availability on our revolving credit facility,” “our debt to total capitalization ratio was 16.3%,” and “our net debt was $117.4 million.” (CFO Tobler)

  • Related-party obligation paydown and remaining balance were disclosed: “we paid down principal of $40.1 million and accrued and current interest of $6.2 million with respect to our related party EB-5 reimbursement obligation,” leaving “approximately $18.5 million due on this obligation.” (CFO Tobler)

  • Hearthstone fee framework was flagged as a potential driver of variability: “you will see some volatility in the implied fee associated with changes in estimates of the performance fees and true-ups when funds or tranches are complete.” (CFO Tobler)

Q&A

  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates LLC: asked about Hearthstone land-banking structure (deposits and payments) and whether builders’ appetite/terms are changing; CFO Tobler: “our land banks use a contract where there's a monthly option payment,” and “No, very consistent. And we're still seeing reasonable interest and progress.” (CFO Tobler)

  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates LLC: asked whether rising fuel prices are pushing inflation into development budgets; President & CEO Hedigan: “We're not actively grading,” “We won't be starting any active grading up in Valencia till the end of the year,” and “Overall, though, we are not seeing any additional increases in our budgets right now for our land development.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts tone was neutral and operationally focused, emphasizing topical investor issues: Ratner said land banking “has been a pretty topical issue that investors are focusing on” and pressed for “any changes in appetite from builders” and “inflation creeping back into your development expenses.” (Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates LLC)

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive but cautious on macro, describing an “unsettled” environment and saying “consumer confidence has been impacted” while also stating, “we believe that markets and demand will recover.” (President & CEO Daniel Hedigan)

  • Versus the prior quarter’s call, management language shifted from record-year framing to market uncertainty; Q4 highlighted “another record year,” while Q1 emphasized being “disciplined and patient” and using balance-sheet flexibility to “protect long-term value.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance level was consistent across both calls: in Q4, management said “we expect consolidated net income in 2026 to be approximately $100 million,” and in Q1 it said, “We are reaffirming our guidance.” (President & CEO Daniel Hedigan)

  • Capital allocation messaging evolved: Q4 discussed the “ability to potentially return capital to shareholders over time,” while Q1 announced a defined authorization, “the $40 million share repurchase.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • Hearthstone milestones progressed from expected fundraising to completed fundraising: Q4 anticipated “$300 million to $500 million of newly originated capital commitments in the first quarter,” while Q1 reported “2 new funds for a total of $600 million in new equity commitments.” (President & CEO Hedigan; COO, Chief Legal Officer & VP Michael Alvarado)

  • Community cadence updates advanced modestly: Q4 Great Park home sales were “78 homes” and Q1 reported “82 homes,” while Valencia moved from “70 new homes” in Q4 to “90 home sales” in Q1; both calls emphasized future program openings and pacing land sales to demand. (President & CEO Hedigan)

Risks and concerns

  • Macro demand risk was a central theme: “The current market environment is unsettled,” with pressures including “increased volatility in financial markets” and “mortgage rates that have risen again recently,” which management said translated into “slower absorption rates” and “a more cautious approach by builders.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • Execution/timing risk remained explicit: “our earnings are inherently tied to the timing of land transactions,” and management again highlighted quarter-to-quarter variability depending on closings. (President & CEO Hedigan)

  • Development and permitting dependencies were noted, particularly for Candlestick: “We're waiting for issuance of final permits to initiate the next phase of land development at Candlestick.” (President & CEO Hedigan)

Final takeaway

Five Point’s first quarter reflected management’s expectation of a low land-sales period, with a $5 million net loss and $13.6 million of revenue largely from Great Park and Hearthstone-related management services. Leadership reaffirmed its full-year 2026 net income target of approximately $100 million with earnings weighted to the second half, citing anticipated land-sale closings and growing Hearthstone fee income. The quarter’s most notable new capital action was the board’s approval of up to a $40 million share repurchase, which management said was designed to preserve liquidity and flexibility while the company navigates an “unsettled” housing market and paces land sales and development activity to protect long-term homesite value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Visteon reaffirms $3.625B-$3.825B 2026 sales outlook amid memory tightness and AI cockpit wins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Visteon (VC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Visteon delivered a solid start to the year with first quarter sales coming ahead of our expectations." (President, CEO & Director Sachin Lawande)

  • "Net sales were $954 million, up 2% year-over-year" and "Growth over market in the quarter was 3%." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • "New business wins were just over $1 billion" and management highlighted "our high-performance compute win with SAIC in China" as "a third customer for AI-based smart cockpit systems." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • "During the quarter, we saw elevated semiconductor costs, while the associated recoveries from customers are expected to be weighted more to the later part of the year." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • "Adjusted free cash flow was negative $23 million" and "we continue to maintain a strong balance sheet with net cash of $385 million." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • "We delivered in Q1 a balanced set of financial results" and "Adjusted EBITDA was $104 million, representing a margin of 10.9%." (Senior VP & CFO Jerome Rouquet)

  • "As we indicated on the prior call, we expected Q1 to be the low point for EBITDA with improvement throughout the year as we make progress on customer recovery agreements and cost initiatives." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

Outlook

  • "We are reaffirming our full year guidance across all key financial metrics" and management attributed this to "the strong start of the year" helping to "offset a softer-than-expected market setup in the second half of the year." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • "We continue to expect revenue in the range of $3.625 billion to $3.825 billion" and "adjusted EBITDA in the range of $455 million to $495 million." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • "We continue to expect adjusted free cash flow in the range of $170 million to $210 million" but "we are currently trending towards the lower end of this range" due to plans "to maintain higher levels of inventory" amid tight supply conditions. (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • "S&P has lowered its global light vehicle production forecast for our customers by approximately 1.5 percentage points" and management said "we are reaffirming our full year sales guidance despite incremental headwinds in the broader market." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

Financial Results

  • "Sales were $954 million, a 2% increase from the prior year" while "customer production volumes were down 4%." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • "Pricing was a headwind of $5 million" and Rouquet said the company used "short-term commercial pricing agreements" while pursuing "longer-term recovery arrangements" to address memory-driven cost inflation. (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • "The additional benefit to sales from onetime settlements, primarily related to EV programs was approximately $20 million, while the EBITDA was approximately $10 million" and Rouquet added that the full-year guidance had included "$10 million of expected one-timers from program settlements, which was achieved in Q1." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • "Adjusted free cash flow for the quarter was negative $23 million" driven by working capital, including that the company "increased inventory levels" and paid "the annual incentive compensation payout" in Q1. (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • "We deployed $40 million in the quarter through share repurchases and dividends" including "$30 million in share repurchases and $10 million in dividends." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

Q&A

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: asked about the second-half production setup and what is baked into guidance; Senior VP & CFO Rouquet said "Q1 came in a little stronger than what we had anticipated" and noted "we were also positively impacted by some EV settlements, about $20 million" while "SAP revised our numbers recently and dropped the second half of the year for us by approximately 2%" but "we do have strong launches" in Q3/Q4 and "overall, a strong H1 with a little bit of a softer H2." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: asked how far along recoveries are for memory costs; President, CEO & Director Lawande said "memory remains constrained" due to AI-driven demand and suppliers shifting away from older nodes, and noted "about 10% of our total full year demand this year for the first time will be met by some of these emerging suppliers"; Senior VP & CFO Rouquet added negotiations are progressing and "we're expecting most of the negotiation to be closed by end of Q2." (President, CEO & Director Lawande; Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: pressed on whether Q1 leakage flips in Q2; Senior VP & CFO Rouquet said "It does. Yes, it does, absolutely" and described improvement through Q3/Q4 with "a slight negative for the full year." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

  • Emmanuel Rosner, Wolfe Research: asked about decontenting to use less memory and whether agreements allow ongoing pass-through; President, CEO & Director Lawande said "we are not actually seeing any interest in decontenting" and described customer-specific structures where "some of them are signing up for a multiyear pricing agreement" while others prefer annual negotiations. (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • Joseph Spak, UBS: asked if OEMs accept China-based memory sourcing; President, CEO & Director Lawande said "in this environment, where there is shortage of parts, there's absolutely no problem with that" and added customers would prefer non-China "if there is availability." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • Dan Levy, Barclays: asked about China dynamics and global OEM share loss; President, CEO & Director Lawande cited "the headwind of the market share loss of the global OEMs" and said "with the HPC launches, I believe we will start to see growth relative to production in China" with "more of a step function next year." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • Gautam Narayan, RBC: asked why M&A is prioritized; President, CEO & Director Lawande tied it to "a very big trend towards a software-driven, more integrated domain controller approach" plus services, scaling smaller expert firms, and "vertical integration" to reduce exposure. (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to pressing, with repeated probing on memory supply, recoveries, and second-half demand, including questions that "keep on getting questions on the DRAM" and multiple follow-ups on leakage timing and sourcing. (Analysts across Goldman Sachs, Wells Fargo, Wolfe, UBS, Barclays)

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive-detail-oriented in Q&A, repeatedly stressing reaffirmation and process control, including "we are reaffirming our full year guidance" and "we are proactively managing supply." (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet; President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • Versus the prior quarter’s framing of memory as "still evolving" with expectations to "cover customer demand" (President, CEO & Director Lawande, Q4 2025), Q1 2026 carried more explicit supply structuring and timing language, including "we expect this environment to persist through 2027" and "we're expecting most of the negotiation to be closed by end of Q2." (President, CEO & Director Lawande; Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance stayed consistent: Q1 2026 reiterated the Q4 2025 full-year ranges (sales "$3.625 billion to $3.825 billion" and adjusted EBITDA "$455 million to $495 million"). (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet; Senior VP & CFO Rouquet in Q4 2025)

  • Memory moved from a disclosed cost headwind assumption in Q4 2025 ("an increase in memory cost... approximately 2% of sales" with "timing mismatch") to execution detail in Q1 2026 ("short-term commercial pricing agreements" and expectation negotiations close "by end of Q2"), with management also tightening the supply narrative around emerging suppliers and qualification. (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet; President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • Q4 2025 emphasized 2025 records ("Adjusted EBITDA reached $492 million" and "record $7.4 billion of new business wins") while Q1 2026 emphasized AI cockpit momentum and pipeline signaling, including that AI cockpit booked value across three OEMs is "already over $1 billion in value." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

Risks and Concerns

  • "S&P has lowered its global light vehicle production forecast" and management flagged downside risk if "the hostilities persist for longer than anticipated." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • "Memory remains constrained" with a "structural supply-demand imbalance" and management said pricing pressure and tightness are expected "through 2027"; mitigation included qualifying additional sources and stating "we were able to secure sufficient supply in Q1" with incremental new-source supply becoming "more meaningful in the second half." (President, CEO & Director Lawande)

  • Cost-recovery timing remained a key execution risk, with Rouquet describing "the timing mismatch of customer recoveries" and an expectation of catch-up and improving commercial dynamics through the year. (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet)

Final Takeaway

Visteon management described Q1 2026 as a stronger-than-expected sales start, reiterated full-year sales, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted free cash flow ranges, and framed near-term investor focus around memory supply tightness, recovery timing through Q2, and second-half production uncertainty tied to revised light-vehicle forecasts. Management also centered the longer-run narrative on AI-based cockpit and high-performance compute wins—highlighting three OEMs and over $1 billion in booked value—while keeping capital deployment priorities unchanged and pointing to the June 25 Investor Day for a broader capital allocation and strategy update.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Robert Half projects Q3 EPS growth of 8% to 12% as Protiviti targets 7% to 9% adjusted segment margins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Robert Half (RHI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Robert Half reported Q1 2026 global enterprise revenues of $1.3 billion, and Vice Chairman, President & CEO M. Waddell said the company was “very pleased that talent solutions delivered a second consecutive quarter of positive sequential growth on a same-day constant-currency basis with revenue trends strengthening as the quarter progressed and into early April.”
  • Waddell said Q1 talent solutions demand improved through March and early April, citing “increased client engagement and higher numbers of job orders, particularly in areas such as technology modernization, data initiatives and IT infrastructure,” while noting decision timelines “remain extended but are beginning to improve.”
  • On Protiviti, Waddell said results were affected by seasonality and “continued shifts in its risk and compliance solutions practice, reflecting ongoing changes in the U.S. financial service regulatory environment,” adding that demand is moving from “large-scale remediation engagements” toward “efficiency-oriented solutions.”
  • Quote (Executive VP & CFO Michael Buckley) “With that in mind, we offer the following second quarter guidance: revenue, $1.275 billion to $1.375 billion; income per share, $0.20 to $0.30; income per share, excluding the $0.03 onetime severance charge, which I'll discuss in a moment, $0.23 to $0.33.”

Outlook

  • Management’s Q2 guidance included revenue of $1.275 billion to $1.375 billion and EPS of $0.20 to $0.30, with Buckley also providing an EPS range excluding the severance charge of $0.23 to $0.33.
  • Buckley said the Q2 midpoint assumes “continued positive adjusted sequential revenue growth for talent solutions,” while Protiviti guidance reflects “ongoing shifts in the U.S. financial services regulatory environment,” and includes planned cost actions with “$5 million in expected severance costs or $0.03 per share,” expected to be “fully completed by the beginning of the third quarter.”
  • For Q3, Buckley provided “general observations,” including that current trends in talent solutions “would result in Q3 year-on-year adjusted revenue growth of 1% to 3%, marking a return to positive growth for the first time since 2022,” and said the company estimates “Q3 consolidated net income and EPS growth of 8% to 12% year-over-year.”

Financial Results

  • In Q1 2026, Robert Half reported global enterprise revenues of $1.3 billion and EPS of $0.14, and Waddell said Q1 EPS was “impacted by a seasonally elevated tax rate, tied to stock-based compensation, which we expect to normalize as the year progresses.”
  • Buckley reported talent solutions revenue declines on an adjusted basis and detailed segment revenue: U.S. talent solutions revenues were $626 million and non-U.S. talent solutions revenues were $208 million; Protiviti global revenues were $466 million with $362 million from the U.S. and $104 million from non-U.S.
  • Buckley reported operating income of $37 million and said adjusted operating income was $29 million, or 2.2% of revenues; he added that Q1 included “an $8 million loss from investments held in employee deferred compensation trusts,” which was “completely offset” and had “no effect on our reported net income.”
  • Buckley said the Q1 tax rate was 56%, and he reported quarter-end accounts receivable of $776 million and DSO of 53.8 days. Waddell reported cash flow used in operations of $112 million and said March dividends totaled $0.59 per share, or $62 million.

Q&A

  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: Asked whether the Q3 talent solutions outlook assumed flat recent weekly revenue trends and whether Protiviti’s Q3 outlook assumed a risk and compliance recovery; (Title Waddell) said “our current run rate is stronger than our Q2 guidance,” adding the company has “not assumed any short-term snapback ... in the risk and compliance practice.”
  • Mark Marcon, Baird: Asked what is driving sequential improvement in talent solutions and whether demand is shifting to temps due to hiring freezes; (Title Waddell) said for SMB clients, “it's more about pent-up demand and really lean resources.”
  • Marcon, Baird: Asked about the $5 million severance action tied to $30 million annual cost reduction; (Title Waddell) said it is “primarily ... directly related to the regulatory enforcement action work.”
  • Andrew Steinerman, JPMorgan: Asked about the “shape of the revenue recovery”; (Title Waddell) cited “pent-up demand” and said the company’s “run rate we've got so far this quarter is greater than our guidance we've given.”
  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO: Asked whether talent solutions is hiring internally; (Title Waddell) said, “We still have our 15% to 30% of capacity in talent solutions. So we're holding the line as we speak.”
  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs: Asked why perm trends looked weaker exiting the quarter while contract improved; (Title Waddell) said perm is “more volatile” short term and management “read very little into kind of 1 month or post quarter.”
  • Tyler Barishaw (for Tobey Sommer), Truist: Asked how long the weaker regulatory environment could last and whether prior Protiviti margin expectations still stand; (Title Waddell) said it could last “the duration of this administration or there's going to be some event,” and added, “given that the headwinds are more than we expected ... just to stay even with last year's margins, I think, is an accomplishment.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on whether improving run rates are sustainable and what Protiviti’s regulatory headwinds imply; examples included questions on whether Q3 assumptions were simply “holding them constant” (Trevor Romeo, William Blair) and whether weakness “is going to last for the duration of the administration” (Tyler Barishaw, Truist).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more assertive in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing improving trends and conservatism in guidance, including (Title Waddell) “we feel the best we felt in a long time,” and “It's simple math. There's no complicated math.”
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on improving macro and sequential growth returning after a multi-year gap, this call’s tone carried more explicit discussion of a specific headwind (U.S. financial services regulatory changes) alongside reiterated confidence in tech-led staffing and consulting demand.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management continued to emphasize sequential improvement in talent solutions: Q4 2025 was described as “the first time in over 3 years” of positive sequential growth, while Q1 2026 was described as “a second consecutive quarter of positive sequential growth” with strengthening trends into early April.
  • Protiviti messaging shifted from Q4’s “modest headwind” in a “more benign” U.S. regulatory environment to Q1’s “marked decline in new enforcement actions,” prompting a defined cost response: “reduce annual cost by $30 million” with a Q2 severance charge.
  • Guidance framing evolved from Q4’s Q1 guide with a “56% to 58%” tax-rate expectation to Q1’s emphasis on Q2 actions and Q3 improvement, including Buckley’s estimate of Q3 EPS growth of 8% to 12% year-over-year.

Risks and Concerns

  • Waddell flagged that “economic uncertainties related to the conflicts in the Middle East and higher energy costs have not yet significantly impacted client demand,” but added that “concerns remain if these conditions persist.”
  • Protiviti’s risk and compliance demand was described as structurally pressured by regulatory change; management’s mitigation was to “assume this is the new normal” and implement cost actions expected to be completed by the beginning of Q3.
  • Waddell said generative AI is increasing application volumes and making candidate verification harder, stating it has “made resumes more homogenous and harder to differentiate,” which management positioned as increasing the need for vetted screening.

Final Takeaway

Robert Half’s management emphasized strengthening sequential trends in talent solutions led by technology-related demand, while outlining a targeted reset at Protiviti’s U.S. risk and compliance practice in response to fewer enforcement-driven engagements. Q2 guidance incorporated a $5 million severance charge tied to a $30 million annual cost reduction, and management pointed to Q3 as a potential inflection point, describing expected positive year-over-year trends and estimating consolidated net income and EPS growth of 8% to 12% year-over-year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Edwards forecasts 9%-11% 2026 sales growth and $2.95-$3.05 adjusted EPS as TAVR guide rises to 7%-9%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Edwards Lifesciences (EW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Building on a year in 2025 marked by solid financial performance and strategic progress, we delivered another strong quarter in Q1, achieving 12.7% sales growth" (CEO Bernard Zovighian), while stating the company is "raising our full year 2026 sales growth guidance to 9% to 11% and adjusted EPS guidance to $2.95 to $3.05" and highlighting longer-term targets: "In 2027 and beyond, we believe our strategy will enable average annual sales growth of approximately 10% as well as operating margin expansion."
  • Zovighian said TAVR Q1 global sales were "$1.2 billion" ("increased 11%" year-over-year) and tied demand to evidence and earlier treatment behavior, adding that Edwards’ "global competitive position in the first quarter increased slightly year-over-year, mainly due to the exit of a competitor in Europe" and that "average selling prices were stable globally."
  • On transcatheter mitral and tricuspid therapies, Zovighian reported Q1 TMTT sales of "$173 million" ("an increase of approximately 42%" year-over-year) and framed the long-term goal: "support our path towards $2 billion of revenue in 2030" alongside 2026 TMTT guidance of "$740 million to $780 million" ("35% to 45% growth").
  • "Total sales of $1.65 billion grew 12.7% year-over-year" and "adjusted EPS of $0.78" (GAAP EPS "$0.66") (CFO Scott Ullem), who also said the company "entered into an accelerated share repurchase agreement to buy back $500 million in shares" and had "approximately $2.4 billion in cash and cash equivalents" at quarter-end.

Outlook

  • Edwards raised full-year guidance: "We are raising our full year total company sales guidance to 9% to 11%, up from 8% to 10%, and our TAVR sales guidance to 7% to 9%, up from 6% to 8%" (CFO Scott Ullem), with updated ranges of "$6.5 billion to $6.9 billion" for total company sales and "$4.7 billion to $5.0 billion" for TAVR sales.
  • For Q2, "we're projecting sales of $1.66 billion to $1.74 billion" and "adjusted earnings per share in Q2 of $0.70 to $0.76" (CFO Ullem).
  • Guidance framing versus the prior call centered on tougher comparisons later in 2026; Ullem reiterated that "our results in 2025 set a higher bar for 2026, especially in the second half."

Financial Results

  • Edwards reported Q1 adjusted gross profit margin of "78.2%" (vs. "78.7%" a year ago) and said the change was driven by "a weakening dollar as well as additional manufacturing expenses related to the expansion of new therapies" (CFO Scott Ullem), while maintaining "full year 78% to 79% gross margin guidance."
  • "Selling, general and administrative expense in the quarter was $522 million or 31.7% of sales" and "research and development expense was $263 million" ("16% of sales") (CFO Ullem), who said the company still expects "2026 R&D as a percentage of sales to be approximately 17%."
  • "First quarter adjusted operating profit margin was 31.4%" (CFO Ullem), and the company expects "full year operating margin to be at the high end of the original 28% to 29% guidance" and reiterated longer-term plans: "In 2027 and beyond, we continue to plan for 50 to 100 basis points of underlying operating margin expansion."
  • Foreign exchange was a notable variable: "Foreign exchange rate changes in the first quarter increased reported sales by approximately $15 million versus guidance" and the company now expects "approximately $55 million upside to full year 2026 sales" from FX (CFO Ullem).

Q&A

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: Asked how competitor long-term data is affecting TAVR selection and guidance; CEO Bernard Zovighian responded, "we produced a ton of data" including "EARLY TAVR," "the 7-year data with Partner 3" and "the 10-year data PARTNER II," saying it "gave extra confidence," while Corporate Vice President Daniel Lippis added, "it's really difficult to parse out exactly what is contributing to what at this time" because the competitor data was "partial" and emerged "mid-late February."
  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: Asked about TMTT’s sequential step-up and early M3 impact; Corporate Vice President Daveen Chopra said "EVOQUE continues to scale up well" and "SAPIEN M3 is the newest kid on the block" with "a lot of physician excitement" because it addresses patients "where they didn't really have a great solution before."
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Pressed on guidance assumptions around share and low-risk competitor data; CEO Zovighian said "the majority of our performance is coming from market growth" and that "everything that you have seen... is in the guidance today," adding, "we try not to focus too much on share because share is a lagging indicator."
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: Asked for an update on SAPIEN X4; Lippis said X4 is "a potential game changer" and that after early clinical use, improvements are going into "a confirmatory trial" with evidence collection "through 2026" before the company comments on "time lines."
  • Travis Steed, BofA: Asked about capacity constraints as new therapies take lab time; CEO Zovighian said, "Capacity has not been an acute matter in the last few quarters," and Chopra added centers are "not taking away from a place like TAVR" and are finding ways to add capacity ("an extra shift" or "an extra lab running").
  • Travis Steed, BofA: Asked about the TAVR NCD outcome and U.S. TAVR growth specificity; CEO Zovighian said, "it is very tough to predict what the final NCD will be" and declined U.S. growth precision: "We don't like to give this kind of specifics."
  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan: Asked why TAVR guidance increased while TMTT was unchanged and about conservatism; CEO Zovighian said the company aims for "a very realistic guidance" and emphasized 2026 comps: "Q1 2025 being low" and "2025 H2 being high" set a "higher bar" for Q3/Q4.
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore: Asked about ASR/share count and weather; CFO Scott Ullem said repurchases totaled "something like $520 million" in Q1 and weather impact was "really nominal."
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore: Asked on PROGRESS trial posture and crossovers; CEO Zovighian said, "we tend not to talk about trial before we can talk about trial results" and that "every one of us are going to discover the trial at TCT."
  • Joanne Wuensch, Citigroup: Asked about expense discipline and CFO succession; CEO Zovighian said R&D prioritization is "very intentional" and that the CFO search "is going well," adding, "as soon as I have news to report, I will say it."
  • Matthew Taylor, Jefferies: Asked if raised TAVR guidance assumes share gains; CEO Zovighian said the increase was "based on our Q1 outperformance" and described Q1 drivers as market growth plus "a little bit of share" (Europe competitor exit and "some slight share gain in the U.S.").
  • Matthew Taylor, Jefferies: Asked about the healthcare environment and coverage; CEO Zovighian said, "we don't see an intense environment" and cited a CMS/FDA policy he called "RAPID" aimed at "early and timely access to care."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive overall, with repeated skepticism directed at how much competitor data and share shifts were embedded in guidance (e.g., questions on "what are you assuming in the guidance from a share standpoint" and requests for U.S. TAVR growth specificity).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks ("another strong quarter") and more guarded in Q&A on competitive dynamics and trial outcomes, using limiting language such as "it's really difficult to parse out" (Corporate Vice President Lippis) and "we tend not to talk about trial before we can talk about trial results" (CEO Zovighian).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s stance on PROGRESS remained consistent—confidence in rationale but constrained pre-read commentary—while Q1 included more direct rebuttals to share-focused framing ("share is a lagging indicator") alongside a guidance raise.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance changed from Q4’s company sales growth of "8% to 10%" and TAVR "6% to 8%" to Q1’s company "9% to 11%" and TAVR "7% to 9%," while TMTT stayed at "$740 million to $780 million" for 2026 in both calls.
  • Q4 emphasized elevated SG&A from patient access initiatives and a Q4 tax-rate issue; Q1 emphasized margin strength ("31.4%" adjusted operating margin) and the phasing of investments ("planned phasing of strategic investments") as contributors to Q1 earnings.
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s lifetime management framework and capital allocation to Q1’s competitor long-term data implications, guidance embedding of share changes, and timing clarity on X4 and the NCD process.

Risks and Concerns

  • Competitive-data uncertainty in TAVR timing and magnitude was acknowledged: "it's really difficult to parse out exactly what is contributing to what at this time" (Corporate Vice President Daniel Lippis), with management positioning guidance as a "realistic" view based on what is known.
  • Policy uncertainty remains around TAVR coverage: "it is very tough to predict what the final NCD will be" (CEO Bernard Zovighian), with the next step cited as a "draft decision memo by June 15."
  • Clinical catalyst risk persists for moderate AS: CEO Zovighian reiterated the company will wait for PROGRESS results at TCT and said, "I don't know anything about the results."

Final Takeaway

Edwards’ management tied Q1’s raised 2026 outlook to broad-based demand and earlier-treatment momentum in structural heart, lifting total company sales growth guidance to 9%-11% and adjusted EPS guidance to $2.95-$3.05 while also raising TAVR growth guidance to 7%-9%. In Q&A, leadership repeatedly framed competitor-driven share questions as secondary to market expansion and evidence generation, stayed non-committal on the final NCD outcome, and maintained a disciplined posture on major clinical readouts (including PROGRESS and the SAPIEN X4 confirmatory work) as the key swing factors investors are pressing for timeline clarity on.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intel outlines Q2 revenue $13.8B-$14.8B and non-GAAP EPS $0.20 as CPU demand outpaces supply
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intel (INTC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Lip-Bu Tan framed Q1 as a supply-constrained upcycle, saying, "Revenue, gross margin, and earnings per share were all above the high end of guidance, marking our sixth consecutive quarter of exceeding financial expectations," while adding, "demand continues to run ahead of supply for all our businesses, especially for Xeon server CPUs."
  • Tan emphasized manufacturing ramps and AI-driven CPU positioning, stating, "Intel 3-based Xeon 6 and Intel 18A based Core Series 3 products are now in full volume production ramp," and argued that "the CPU is reinserting itself as the indispensable foundation of the AI era" as customers rebalance CPU-to-accelerator configurations.
  • Tan highlighted multiple partnerships, including, "Our recent announcement with SambaNova Systems," and said, "We recently announced our partnership with SpaceX, xAI and Tesla to support Terafab," describing work to "explore innovative ways to refactor silicon process technology."
  • CFO David Zinsner attributed Q1 outperformance to supply and mix, saying, "We delivered robust Q1 results, reflecting strong demand and better-than-expected available supply," and reported, "First quarter revenue was $13.6 billion."
  • Zinsner detailed profitability and cash flow, stating, "Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 41%," "non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.29," and "Q1 operating cash flow was $1.1 billion" with "gross CapEx of $5 billion" and "adjusted free cash flow of minus $2 billion."

Outlook

  • Zinsner guided Q2 revenue to "a range of $13.8 billion to $14.8 billion" and said, "At the midpoint of $14.3 billion, we forecast a gross margin of 39%, a tax rate of 11% and EPS of $0.20, all on a non-GAAP basis."
  • He cautioned on macro and input costs, noting "constraints and rising prices around key components like memory, wafers and substrates," and said Intel is "prudently planning for PC demand to weaken in the second half of the year" with "the full year PC unit TAM to be down low double-digit percent."
  • Against that, Zinsner tightened commentary on servers, stating, "Our outlook for server CPU demand has improved over the last 90 days," and projecting "a strong year of double-digit unit growth for the industry and for us with momentum extending into 2027."
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the tone shifted from "our internal supply constraints are most acute in Q1" (prior call) to Q1 2026 guidance built around sequential growth in both CCG and DCAI on improving supply, while also calling out a Q2 gross margin step-down driven by early-node mix.

Financial Results

  • Zinsner said Q1 results benefited from pricing and inventory actions: "We also benefited from improved product mix and pricing actions," and "Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 41%," including "previously reserved inventory" and "better yields on Intel 18A" offsetting early-ramp costs.
  • Segment disclosures centered on supply and AI mix: CCG revenue was "$7.7 billion, down 6% sequentially," with "AI PC revenue grew 8% sequentially and now represents greater than 60% of our client CPU mix," while DCAI revenue was "$5.1 billion, an increase of 7% sequentially and 22% year-over-year."
  • Intel Foundry remained a major drag as revenue rose on EUV mix but losses persisted: "Intel Foundry delivered revenue of $5.4 billion, up 20% sequentially," "External foundry revenue was $174 million," and "Intel Foundry operating loss in Q1 was $2.4 billion," which Zinsner said should improve as "18A continues to ramp into volume and yields improve further."
  • Zinsner updated capital and balance sheet positioning, saying 2026 capex is now "flat to last year versus our prior expectation of flat to down," and described a Fab 34 Ireland buyout: "we funded our purchase with approximately $7.7 billion in cash and $6.5 billion in new debt."

Q&A

  • Benjamin Reitzes, Melius Research LLC: asked about long-term agreements and visibility; CEO Tan replied, "Google is one of the multi- long-term contract agreement in Q1," and CFO Zinsner added, "most of these agreements are structured with volume and pricing" and "usually somewhere between 3 and 5 years."
  • Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank AG: pressed on server CPU competitiveness and whether higher capex is needed to scale supply; Tan said the CPU-to-GPU ratio is shifting as "it used to be 1 and 8, and now it's 1:4," while emphasizing yield and cycle-time work and customer decisions tied to PDK maturity.
  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: challenged the Q2 margin setup; Zinsner cited "Panther Lake volume increases" and said 18A is "a pretty decent headwind" as volumes rise, while also flagging "memory going up" and "substrates are going up" as second-half offsets.
  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: asked how much demand is being missed; Zinsner declined to quantify but said, "Let's just say it starts with a B. So it's meaningful," and Tan stayed non-specific on Terafab structure: "we would update you as we go."
  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about output levers and advanced packaging scale; Zinsner said Intel is "increasing wafer starts" across nodes and described packaging demand as "in the billions of dollars per year kind of level."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical in spots, with Rasgon saying, "I'm just a little confused" on gross margin drivers and repeatedly pressing on PC demand shape.
  • Management tone was positive but guarded on constraints and costs, with Zinsner repeatedly emphasizing supply limits and input inflation, and Tan using confidence language such as "I'm confident" and "stay tuned" while avoiding specifics on unannounced contracts.
  • Versus Q4 2025, management messaging moved from a Q1 trough narrative (prior quarter) to stronger confidence, including Tan’s line, "A year ago, the conversation about Intel was about whether we could survive. Today, it's about how quickly we can add manufacturing capacity."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 featured a sharper supply-and-demand imbalance narrative than Q4 2025, with Tan now stating, "demand continues to run ahead of supply for all our businesses," while Q4 emphasized Q1 being the most acute constraint period.
  • Guidance language improved sequentially from Q4’s Q1 outlook of "$11.7 billion to $12.7 billion" revenue and "breakeven EPS" to Q1’s Q2 guide that includes non-GAAP EPS of "$0.20," while explicitly warning that Q1 inventory benefits "aren't expected to repeat in Q2."
  • Foundry commentary evolved from customer-decision timing on 14A in the "second half of this year" (Q4) to Q1 specifics on PDK progress and expected "earlier design commitments" beginning in "the second half of 2026" and into "the first half of 2027."

Risks and Concerns

  • Zinsner warned that "macroeconomic and geopolitical environments are dynamic" and highlighted "rising prices" in memory and substrates as potential demand and margin headwinds.
  • Management repeatedly cited supply constraints as a limiter, with Zinsner saying Q1 revenue "would have been meaningfully higher" and later cautioning that certain Q1 inventory sales are "not sure we have that benefit" in Q2.
  • PC demand risk was called out directly: Intel is "prudently planning for PC demand to weaken in the second half of the year" even as near-term orders remain "very robust."

Final Takeaway

Tan and Zinsner portrayed Q1 as an execution-driven quarter with demand still ahead of supply, anchored by a rebound in server CPU momentum and a fast ramp of Intel 18A products that helped Q1 profitability but is expected to pressure Q2 gross margin as volumes rise. Management’s Q2 framework pointed to sequential growth across key segments alongside heightened caution on second-half PC demand and escalating input costs, while Q&A centered on long-term customer agreements, the magnitude of unmet demand, and whether yield and cycle-time improvements can translate into sustained supply expansion and improving foundry economics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
